codeSnapshot.label: adagrad.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

model = nn.Linear(10, 1)

# PyTorch AdaGrad
optimizer = torch.optim.Adagrad(
    model.parameters(),
    lr=0.01,
    lr_decay=0,        # optional LR decay per step
    weight_decay=0,    # L2 regularisation
    eps=1e-10          # numerical stability
)

# AdaGrad update rule:
# G_t = G_{t-1} + g_t^2   (accumulate squared gradients, per parameter)
# w_t = w_{t-1} - η / sqrt(G_t + ε) * g_t

# Manual implementation
class AdaGrad:
    def __init__(self, params, lr=0.01, eps=1e-10):
        self.params = list(params)
        self.lr = lr
        self.eps = eps
        self.G = [torch.zeros_like(p) for p in self.params]

    def step(self):
        for p, G in zip(self.params, self.G):
            if p.grad is None: continue
            G.add_(p.grad ** 2)  # accumulate squared gradients
            p.data.sub_(self.lr * p.grad / (G.sqrt() + self.eps))

# Demonstrate diminishing learning rate problem:
# After many steps, G grows large → effective lr → 0
import torch
G = torch.zeros(1)
for t in range(1, 201):
    g = torch.tensor([1.0])  # constant gradient for illustration
    G += g**2
    eff_lr = 1.0 / (G.sqrt() + 1e-10)
    if t in [1, 10, 50, 100, 200]:
        print(f"Step {t:3d}: G={G.item():.1f}, eff_lr={eff_lr.item():.4f}")

eli3: AdaGrad adapts the learning rate for each parameter based on how frequently that parameter has been updated. Parameters that have received many large gradient updates get a smaller learning rate (they've already learned a lot). Parameters that rarely get updated keep a higher learning rate (they still have a lot to learn).

This was designed for problems where some features appear much more often than others — like word frequencies in text. Common words ('the', 'a') appear in every sentence and get huge gradient updates. Rare words need to learn from the few times they appear. AdaGrad handles this automatically.

The downside: AdaGrad never forgets. The accumulated sum of squared gradients only grows, so the learning rate only shrinks — eventually reaching near zero. RMSProp and Adam fix this by using exponential moving averages instead of cumulative sums.

whenWhy.title: Sparse features in NLP and recommendation systems
whenWhy.body: In logistic regression or shallow embedding models for text/recommendation, some features (words, user IDs) appear millions of times while others appear once. AdaGrad gives rare features a high effective learning rate and common features a low one. This is ideal for convex problems with sparse data, such as linear classifiers trained on bag-of-words features.

whenWhy.title: Why AdaGrad is rarely used in deep learning
whenWhy.body: In deep networks trained for hundreds of epochs, the accumulated G_t grows unboundedly → effective learning rate → 0. Training effectively stops before convergence. The same problem makes AdaGrad unsuitable for non-convex optimisation where you need sustained updates throughout training. RMSProp (decaying average of G_t) and Adam (first + second moment with bias correction) fix this issue.

whenWhy.title: Convex optimisation with sparse gradients
whenWhy.body: AdaGrad has provable regret bounds for online convex optimisation with sparse gradients: O(√T) vs O(T) for SGD. Duchi et al. (2011) proved that AdaGrad achieves the optimal O(d · ln T) bound for d-dimensional sparse problems. For convex shallow models (linear regression, logistic regression, shallow embeddings) trained once, AdaGrad outperforms SGD and avoids manual learning rate tuning.

deepDiveIntro: AdaGrad (Duchi et al., 2011) maintains a running sum of squared gradients for each parameter separately. The effective learning rate for each parameter shrinks in proportion to how much gradient it has accumulated — parameters that have received many large gradient updates slow down, while rarely-updated parameters maintain a higher learning rate. [1]

This per-parameter scaling approximates the diagonal of the Fisher information matrix, which captures how sensitive the loss is in each weight direction. Using only the diagonal is a memory-efficient approximation — the full Fisher matrix would require storing and inverting a matrix the size of the number of parameters squared. [1]

deepDive.heading: Update Rule
deepDive.body: AdaGrad accumulates the squared gradient for each parameter separately across all training steps. The weight update divides the gradient by the square root of this accumulated sum, so parameters with a large history of large gradients get a proportionally smaller step. The key limitation: this accumulated sum only ever grows, so the effective learning rate for every parameter eventually decays toward zero — training can stall before the model has converged. [1]
deepDive.formula: Gₜ = Gₜ₋₁ + gₜ²;  wₜ = wₜ₋₁ − η·gₜ/√(Gₜ+ε)

deepDive.heading: AdaGrad vs RMSProp: The Dying Learning Rate Fix
deepDive.body: AdaGrad's accumulated gradient sum grows forever, so its effective learning rate only ever decreases. RMSProp fixes this by using an exponential moving average instead of a cumulative sum — old gradients gradually fade out, so the effective learning rate settles at a stable value rather than decaying to zero. This makes RMSProp suitable for long training runs and non-stationary problems. AdaGrad remains useful in settings where you actually want the learning rate to decrease over time, such as online convex optimization with sparse data. [2]

deepDive.heading: Full-Matrix AdaGrad
deepDive.body: The theoretically ideal version of AdaGrad uses the full matrix of gradient products rather than just the diagonal. This accounts for correlations between parameters and provides optimal preconditioning. In practice it is completely infeasible for large models: storing the matrix requires space proportional to the square of the number of parameters, and inverting it is even more expensive. Methods like K-FAC and Shampoo approximate this using structured matrix factorizations that are cheaper to store and compute. [1]

takeaways: Gₜ = Gₜ₋₁ + gₜ²; wₜ = wₜ₋₁ − η·gₜ/√(Gₜ+ε) — per-parameter adaptive learning rate. [1]
Effective lr ∝ 1/√(accumulated squared gradient) — decays to 0, main limitation. [1]
Optimal for sparse convex problems: O(√T) regret bound for sparse gradients. [1]
RMSProp fixes dying lr by using exponential moving average of g² instead of cumulative sum. [2]
Rarely used in deep learning — Adam (= AdaGrad + momentum + EMA fix) is preferred. [2]

edgeCases.q: In what setting is AdaGrad provably better than SGD?
edgeCases.a: AdaGrad achieves regret O(Σᵢ ‖gₜ,ᵢ‖₂ · √T) = O(d^(1/2) · ‖g‖₁ · √T) for sparse problems. When gradients are sparse (many zero entries), ‖g‖₁ << d^(1/2) ‖g‖₂, and AdaGrad's bound is much tighter than SGD's O(‖g‖₂ √T). Duchi et al. proved this is optimal up to logarithmic factors for online convex optimisation with subgradients. In practice: training word embeddings, logistic regression on text features, or linear models on high-dimensional sparse data are the right use cases.

edgeCases.q: What is ε in AdaGrad and how does it affect training?
edgeCases.a: ε prevents division by zero when G_t,i = 0 (parameter i has never received a gradient). PyTorch default ε = 1e-10, which is much smaller than Adam's 1e-8. With ε = 1e-10, the first gradient update for an unused parameter is: Δw = η·g/√(g² + 1e-10) ≈ η·sign(g) — almost a full step. With larger ε (e.g., ε = 0.1), the effective learning rate is bounded by η/√ε even for small G — provides more conservative initial updates. For fp16 training, ε < 6e-5 may underflow — use ε = 1e-7 or 1e-6.

sources.label: Duchi, Hazan & Singer (2011) — Adaptive Subgradient Methods for Online Learning and Stochastic Optimization, JMLR 12:2121–2159
Goodfellow, Bengio & Courville — Deep Learning, Ch. 8
